hook: The pasha Glaoui's palace, now a museum and a famous coffee bar
facts: Built 1910 as the residence of Thami El Glaoui, pasha of Marrakech from 1912
Glaoui ran the Moroccan south under French rule for over four decades
Reopened as the Musee des Confluences in December 2017
Houses an outpost of the Bacha Coffee house in its courtyard
w: Thami El Glaoui, pasha and warlord of the south, threw parties here that drew Colette, Ravel, Chaplin and Churchill while he carved up Morocco for the French. The strongman fell with the protectorate; his house reopened in 2017 as a museum of cultures, with a coffee bar in the old salons. Power, gilded then forgotten.

hook: Carved Almohad gate into the royal kasbah, eight centuries weathered
facts: Completed around 1188-1190 under the Almohad caliph Yaqub al-Mansur
Served as the grand public entrance to the southern royal kasbah
Framed by a Kufic Quranic frieze from Surah al-Hijr in carved stone
Name 'agnaou' thought to be Berber, variously read as 'mutes'
w: The Almohads cut this gate into the kasbah wall around 1190, ringing the arch with concentric carved stone and a Kufic Quran frieze instead of the usual military bulk. No towers, just ornament; a ceremonial door for a caliph. Salts and city air are eating the upper stone now. Eight hundred years, slowly dissolving.

hook: Late-16th-century Saadian house, now Marrakech's first literary cafe
facts: Dates to the second half of the 16th century, the Saadian period
One of very few well-preserved Saadian houses left in the medina
Carved cedar and stucco echo the contemporary Ben Youssef Madrasa
Restored around 2000 as a salon de the, art gallery and cultural centre
w: Down a dead-end alley near the Mouassine mosque, this Saadian merchant's house has carried its carved cedar and stucco since the late 1500s, when craftsmen who built Ben Youssef worked the same designs here. Saved from ruin around 2000, it pours mint tea among books and art now. Four centuries of quiet, off a noisy souk.

hook: The Thursday Gate, fronting a sprawling junk-and-flea market
facts: Almoravid gate dating to around 1126, once called Bab Fes
Named for the al-Khamis (Thursday) market once held outside it
Heavily rebuilt in 1803-04 under Sultan Moulay Slimane
A marabout's domed tomb stands just outside the gate
w: The 'Thursday Gate' took its name from a market that once met here weekly; now the trading spills across most days, a chaos of salvaged doors, mismatched chairs, old radios and other people's discarded lives. Almoravid walls, an 1800s rebuild, a saint's tomb out front. Marrakech's junk heap, and its treasure hunt.

hook: Saadian Friday mosque founded by a sultan's formidable mother
facts: Built 1557-1571 under the Saadian sultan Abdallah al-Ghalib
Commissioned by Lalla Mas'uda, mother of sultan Ahmad al-Mansur
Also called Jami al-Hurra, 'Mosque of the Free Woman'
Anchored a charitable complex with madrasa, hammam, library and fountain
w: Lalla Messaouda, a sultan's wife and the mother of Morocco's great gold-bringing king, raised this Friday mosque in the 1560s and endowed it with a madrasa, hammam, library and fountain. They nicknamed it the Mosque of the Free Woman, for her. The fondouks she also built have vanished; her mosque still calls the hour.

hook: Whitewashed tomb of the woman saint who became a dove, by the Koutoubia
facts: Tomb of Fatima Zohra bint al-Kush, a mystic of the early 17th century
Legend makes her the daughter of a freed sub-Saharan slave turned imam
Square white koubba with scalloped crenellations beside the Koutoubia
Revered as a patron of women; mothers still bring daughters for blessing
legend: She is said to have turned into a dove at night and back to a woman at dawn
Believed to grant baraka and to have worked miracles
w: A small white dome in the Koutoubia's shadow holds Lalla Zohra, a mystic remembered as the daughter of a freed slave. Legend says she turned into a dove each night and back to a woman at dawn. Patron of the city's women, who still bring their daughters to the scalloped walls for her blessing. Everyone walks past; few stop.

hook: A small museum for Berber rag-rugs knotted from worn-out clothes
facts: Opened 2014 in a 19th-century riad called Dardala, off Derb el Cadi
Founded by French collector Patrick de Maillard, who still curates it
Shows boucharouite rugs hand-knotted from recycled cotton and synthetics
Named for the Berber word for these scrap-cloth carpets
w: Down the alleys behind Ben Youssef, a collector turned an old riad over to the poor woman's carpet: the boucharouite, knotted not from costly wool but from strips of worn-out clothes and rags. Loud, improvised, gorgeous. A whole museum of what village women made from nothing. Other people's old shirts, rewoven into colour.

hook: Almohad royal olive groves around a vast pool where a sultan drowned
facts: Laid out by the Almohad caliph Abd al-Mu'min around 1157
The huge Dar al-Hana basin, fed by mountain channels, dates to that era
Stretches over hundreds of hectares of olive and orchard south of the city
A pavilion, the Dar el-Hana, overlooks the largest reservoir
legend: Sultan Mohammed IV is said to have drowned here in 1873 when his steam launch capsized
w: The Almohads watered these royal olive groves from the mountains in the 1150s, filling a basin so wide that troops trained to swim in it. They say Sultan Mohammed IV died in that same pool in 1873, drowned when his pleasure launch capsized. Centuries of orchard and still water, with one famous bad afternoon.

hook: Boccara's domed opera house, half-built and waiting decades on
facts: Designed by architect Charles Boccara, begun after 1970s planning
Opened in 2001: only the exterior and a 1,200-seat amphitheatre finished
The 800-seat opera hall inside stays a raw concrete shell, unfinished
Stands at the head of Avenue Mohammed VI, opposite the railway station
w: Charles Boccara wrapped a Roman cupola and columns around an opera house that, decades on, has never sung. The king inaugurated the shell and the outdoor amphitheatre in 2001; the 800-seat hall inside remains raw concrete, a cavern waiting for an audience. Grand facade, hollow heart. Gueliz's beautiful unfinished thought.

hook: The House of Storks, a former bird hospital turned arts foundation
facts: Name means 'House of the Storks'; built 1930s over an old funduq
The earlier funduq reputedly served as a hospital for injured storks
Rescued in the 1990s by Swiss architects Susanna Biedermann and Max Alioth
Runs Sufi concerts, calligraphy and craft workshops near Ben Youssef
w: The 'House of Storks' takes its name from the funduq that once stood here, where, the story goes, the city's injured storks were nursed back to flight. A pair of Swiss architects pulled the ruin back from collapse in the 1990s and filled it with Sufi music, calligraphy and craft. A bird hospital, reborn as a house of culture.

hook: Tinsmiths' square at the Mellah's edge, ringing with hammers
facts: Sits between El Badi and Bahia palaces, on the rim of the old Mellah
The Mellah was laid out by the Saadians in 1558 on former royal stables
Workshops still beat lanterns and lamps from sheet metal by hand
Recently pedestrianised around a central fountain
w: On the edge of the Mellah, where the Saadians settled the city's Jews on the site of old royal stables in 1558, the tinsmiths still work. Lanterns and lamps take shape under hammers in stalls around the square, the same metallic clinking heard here for centuries. Bankers and jewellers once; lamp-beaters now, father to son.
